content:
AUDIENCE:  
This reader is an adult who is naturally skeptical, perhaps scientifically minded or simply pragmatic. They likely distrust exaggerated claims about supernatural or extraordinary phenomena, suspecting hype or misinformation. They value clear, logical explanations and real-world evidence, and dislike overly sentimental or melodramatic writing. They care about practical implications and authenticity, and may be wary of stories that seem implausible or overly fantastical without grounding. They would find vague, mystical language boring or frustrating, and appreciate a tone that is straightforward, a bit dry, and acknowledges skepticism while still engaging imagination. The story should avoid preaching or emotional manipulation and instead offer a plausible, thought-provoking scenario.

===BEGIN===  
At the park with my kids, I noticed the sudden crackle in the air—a sharp smell, a blue flash darting between trees. Lightning was about to strike. I grabbed my children and pulled them away from the clearing just as a bolt hit the ground where we stood seconds before. The shock knocked me down. 

When I came to, something strange had changed. My senses—the way I noticed the smallest details, sounds, and smells—had ramped up dramatically, almost like my brain had been rewired. It wasn’t magic or superstition, just an odd side effect of the strike, something I can’t fully explain yet. 

Skeptical as I am, I can’t deny it: the world feels sharper, clearer, more immediate. Whether it lasts or what it means, I’m still figuring that out. But sometimes, reality surprises even the most doubtful.  
===END===
